skill.name: memory-management
skill.description: Guides Claude Code and Codex users to persist project preferences, decisions, corrections, and context in transparent local memory files.
skill.summary: Manage durable project memory for preferences, decisions, corrections, and context refreshes.
skill.author: DojoCodingLabs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and weak cryptography hits, but review found these are markdown examples, file paths, and slash-command references rather than executable code. The main real risk is privacy exposure from persistent local memory files if users ignore the skill guidance and store secrets or sensitive project data.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Local Memory Can Expose Sensitive Context
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to store preferences, decisions, corrections, and project knowledge in .claude/memory files. It repeatedly warns not to store secrets, but the persistence model can still retain sensitive context if users or agents save inappropriate data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes durable local memory storage and names sensitive categories that must not be stored. This is a legitimate feature, but the privacy risk is real if the guidance is not followed.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged locations are markdown paths, fenced JSON or TypeScript examples, slash-command references, and sample workflow text. No executable script, shell invocation, Ruby backtick expression, or command runner was found in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows documentation examples and literal command names, not runnable code. The skill repository shard contains only SKILL.md, so there is no hidden script implementing these examples.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged weak-crypto locations occur in ordinary prose about Claude Code, corrections, decisions, and user feedback. No hashing, encryption, signing, or cryptographic API usage was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are natural-language documentation and list items. They do not reference MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic operation.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is a Documentation False Positive
low_findings.description: The flagged line advises users not to assume stored memory is always valid and to check staleness. It does not enumerate host details, collect system data, or run reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a best-practice warning about stale memory. There is no code or instruction to inspect the host system beyond normal project-memory maintenance.

content.value_statement: Development sessions lose valuable context when preferences and decisions are not recorded. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a clear process for safe project memory.
actual_capabilities: Defines what project preferences, decisions, corrections, and knowledge should be remembered.
Provides storage patterns for visible .claude/memory files organized by project and skill.
Explains when to persist immediately, defer persistence, or avoid persistence entirely.
Shows how to track repeated user corrections and convert them into learned preferences.
Documents memory refresh strategies for stale dependencies, project changes, and user resets.
Includes user-facing commands for viewing, clearing, refreshing, exporting, and importing memory.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not implement memory commands by itself.
It depends on agents and users following the rule to avoid sensitive data.
It does not define a complete validation schema for every memory file.
It assumes a Claude-style local memory directory is acceptable for the project.

use_cases.title: Preserve Team Coding Preferences
use_cases.description: Record repeated choices such as import style, naming conventions, and error handling so future sessions apply them consistently.

use_cases.title: Explain Architecture Decisions Later
use_cases.description: Capture decisions, rationale, alternatives, impact, and trade-offs so maintainers can understand why an approach was chosen.

use_cases.title: Reduce Repeated Corrections
use_cases.description: Track recurring user feedback and convert confirmed patterns into future behavior that needs less manual correction.

prompt_templates.title: Save a Preference
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a clear user preference.
prompt_templates.prompt: Remember that this project uses named imports. Store it only if it is safe and visible to me.

prompt_templates.title: Log a Decision
prompt_templates.scenario: Record an architecture choice with rationale.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a decision log entry for choosing JWT authentication. Include rationale, alternatives, impact, and follow-up risks.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Memory
prompt_templates.scenario: Check whether stored project memory is still useful.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the project memory for stale assumptions. Identify entries that should be refreshed, confirmed, or removed.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Memory Policy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced setup for a team workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a project memory policy for this repository. Define what to store, what to reject, update triggers, and review cadence.

output_examples.input: Remember that we use named imports after I corrected it twice.
output_examples.output: The assistant records a high-confidence preference, notes that it came from repeated corrections, and states where the memory is stored.

output_examples.input: We decided to use Supabase Edge Functions for backend tasks.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a decision summary with the date, rationale, alternatives, impact, and affected areas.

output_examples.input: Refresh memory after the package upgrade.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks project-memory entries tied to dependencies and marks outdated assumptions for confirmation or removal.

best_practices: Store only preferences, decisions, corrections, and project context that will improve future work.
Keep memory files visible, readable, and easy for users to clear or refresh.
Add timestamps, sources, confidence levels, and expiry rules to reduce stale assumptions.
anti_patterns: Do not store API keys, tokens, personal data, private URLs, or sensitive business logic.
Do not persist temporary experiments, generic best practices, or information already captured in code.
Do not apply inferred preferences forever without confidence checks and refresh rules.

faq.question: Does this skill implement memory storage automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples for agents to create transparent local memory files.

faq.question: Where does the skill recommend storing memory?
faq.answer: It recommends visible files under .claude/memory, organized by project and skill.

faq.question: Can it store secrets or credentials?
faq.answer: No. The guidance explicitly says not to store API keys, tokens, credentials, or sensitive business data.

faq.question: How does it learn from corrections?
faq.answer: It tracks repeated corrections and treats confirmed patterns as preferences with confidence metadata.

faq.question: How should stale memory be handled?
faq.answer: Memory should be refreshed when dependencies, project structure, technology choices, or user preferences change.

faq.question: Is this useful outside Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can use the same concepts for transparent project context management.
